topic: A tenant receives an eviction notice
raw_output:
[story 1]

The crisp white paper lay on the kitchen counter, mocking me with its official seal. My hands began to tremble so violently that the sugar bowl rattled on the shelf. I stared at the bolded words—'Notice of Termination of Tenancy'—as if they were an alien language I was utterly incapable of decoding. A hot, prickly sensation crawled up my neck, making my breath catch in my throat. I backed away from the counter, bumping into the refrigerator, and let out a sound that was half-gasp, half-choked sob. Every shadow in the hallway seemed to writhe, and the air in the small apartment suddenly felt too thick, too heavy to draw a full breath. I started pacing, my footsteps quick and uneven on the linoleum floor, unable to focus on anything but the stark declaration before me.

[story 2]

"No. No, this can't be happening." I whispered the words, my voice thin and reedy, barely audible over the distant hum of traffic outside. I snatched the envelope, crumpling the edges instinctively, the paper tearing under my grip. My vision tunneled, the edges of the room blurring into streaks of panicked color. My heart hammered against my ribs with such force I thought it might crack a bone. I dropped the notice onto the worn armchair, and then I began to shake, a full-body tremor that made my legs feel like jelly. I squeezed my eyes shut, trying to force the images away—the landlord’s stern face, the cold finality of the date—but they were all imprinted behind my eyelids, swirling in a dizzying vortex of dread.

[story 3]

I stood frozen in the doorway, clutching the eviction paper to my chest as if it were a live wire. My palms were slick with a cold sweat, and the room spun slowly, the familiar wallpaper seeming to ripple and distort. A high, thin sound escaped my lips—a strangled noise that was definitely not human. I took a staggering step backward, my eyes wide and unfocused, darting around the cramped living space as if expecting the notice to materialize from the shadows. My jaw locked tight, and I could feel the pressure building behind my eyes, a sharp, painful tightening that made focusing impossible. I began to rock back and forth, a frantic, uncontrolled motion, unable to anchor myself to the solid reality of the room.
